sp.p: (embracing Nell.) Kiss me, child! Pretty darling! what wit she has ! Oh, Nelly, how may one woman be deceived in another! Now, the stars pardon me my bad words to you !

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: I forgive them ; only tell me how you came here ?

sp.speaker: Moll.
sp.p: I suffered myself to be led away in your name. Oh, my dear baby ! you don't know the wickedness of this town— I do. I was shown into that room, where I must have fallen asleep. But how, my innocent, did you come here ?

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: I thought I was going home, when I was cheated to this place.

sp.speaker: Moll.
sp.p: Cheated! Well, let's be friends; though you take half my orange custom at the theatre.

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: Never fear ; I am no longer your rival. I have obtained all I ventured for; for to-day I speak a prologue at the theatre!

sp.speaker: Moll.
sp.p: A prologue! why—hush !—

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: The counsellor! Hide, and this time leave him to me.

sp.speaker: Moll.
sp.p: But, my dear angel, the gown—

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: Gown and all shall be yours. Quick ! (Moll retires into room, R. 2 E.)

div.stage: Enter Crowsfoot with a square deal box—he fastens door, R. 1 E.

sp.speaker: Crows.
sp.p: Here it is, Nelly! What, (puts box down.) taken off thy mask ? that's well.

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: What gown can it be ?

sp.speaker: Crows.
sp.p: What gown? the gown you admired at Madame Charrett's!

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: Why, you have never bought it ?

sp.speaker: Crows.
sp.p: Bought it to surprise you; and head-gear to suit. Thou shalt dress like an empress, Nelly. See, (taking gown from box.) here's the gown! (Moll comes down between Nell and Crowsfoot.) Here it is, be happy and take it.

sp.speaker: Moll.
sp.p: (twitching it away.) I will!

sp.speaker: Crows.
sp.p: In the devil's name, What witch is this?

sp.speaker: Moll.
sp.p: (taking off her mask.) Witch! no more a witch than thou'rt a conjuror.

sp.speaker: Crows.
sp.p: (R.) That hag of the pit. Orange Moll!

sp.speaker: Moll.
sp.p: Hag! "why thou superannuated pounce box! , thou piece of faded red tape! thou nothing made something by a wig! Hag! pah!

sp.speaker: Crows.
sp.p: I shall go off in a spasm ! how got she here?

sp.speaker: Moll.
sp.p: How? and don't the blushes burn your wrinkles to ask? Wasn't I carried off?

sp.speaker: Crows.
sp.p: I—I— Come, Nelly, 'tis nearly the time that—the time—what's o'clock ? (looking doubtfully at Nell and Molly.

sp.speaker: Moll.
sp.p: (taking out watch.) Seven minutes to two, by the watch of your dear first wife, (putting it back carefully.)

sp.speaker: Crows.
sp.p: Watch! ring! robbery! Jade, I'll hang you—I'll—

div.stage: (violent knocking at door, R. 1 E.

sp.speaker: Stockfish.
sp.p: (without.) Counsellor—counsellor! There'll be murder.

sp.speaker: Crows.
sp.p: Is Beelzebub making holiday ? what next ?

div.stage: (Nell opens door—knocking without.
Enter Stockfish, R. 1 E.

sp.speaker: Stock.
sp.p: Flee! flee, or ye are a dead mad! The Shropshire wagon is come in !

sp.speaker: Crows.
sp.p: D—n the Shropshire wagon! what of that?

sp.speaker: Stock.
sp.p: And in it seven stout young men, who clamour and cry for you.

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: For the counsellor—for what?

sp.speaker: Stock.
sp.p: For their sister, whom, as they complain, he hath conveyed away.

sp.speaker: Crows.
sp.p: (aside.) Shropshire! can I by accident have stumbled on the truth ? Why, Nelly, where are you from ?

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: (R.) Shropshire, sir!

sp.speaker: Moll.
sp.p: (L.) I'm Shropshire too !

sp.speaker: Crows.
sp.p: (to Nell.) And have you any brothers?

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: Seven.

sp.speaker: Moll.
sp.p: Just my number.

sp.speaker: Stock.
sp.p: Savage and cruel do they look—and they row wrathfully against thy bones.

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: Just like 'em—my brothers are dragoons.

sp.speaker: Moll.
sp.p: So are mine! Brother Tom once killed a butcher!

sp.speaker: Stock.
sp.p: Some of them do carry cudgels as thick as my arm, and some—

sp.speaker: Crows.
sp.p: Well?

sp.speaker: Stock.
sp.p: A little thicker! If they do find ye with their sister, they will slaughter ye.

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: (clinging to Crowsfoot.) But I'll never leave you.

sp.speaker: Moll.
sp.p: (clinging to him) No —nor I—never.

sp.speaker: Crows.
sp.p: (trying to shake them off.) You shall leave me ! I don't want you ! I don't know !

div.stage: (knocking without R.

sp.speaker: Haynes.
sp.p: (without, R.) We will go up—we'll ha' his life !

sp.speaker: Stock.
sp.p: What a shocking thing 'twill be—

sp.speaker: Crows.
sp.p: What?

sp.speaker: Stock.
sp.p: To have a crowner's inquest at the Mitre!

div.stage: (knocking continued—voices without, R.

sp.speaker: Voices.
sp.p: We won't be stopped—we'll ha' his life!

sp.speaker: Crows.
sp.p: Put me anywhere! do anything with me! My character ! my bones!

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: The only chance, counsellor—get into the gown!

div.stage: (taking gown from box.

sp.speaker: Crows.
sp.p: What! turn woman !

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: Or be cudgelled for a man.

sp.speaker: Crows.
sp.p: Give it to me! oh, that I were in the Temple!

div.stage: (Nell and Moll hastily dress Crowsfoot in gown and head-dress—knocking continued.

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: There—pull this well over your head.

sp.speaker: Moll.
sp.p: And be sure to walk pretty and tripping like one of us.

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: Here they come. (Stockfish whispers Nell.) A trick, indeed!

div.stage: Enter Joe Haynes, and two others disguised in smock frocks, as Peasants, at door, R. 1 E.

sp.speaker: Haynes.
sp.p: (speaking as he enters.) Giles, Dick, and John— stay there and watch below.

sp.speaker: All.
sp.p: Where be he—where be he ?

sp.speaker: Haynes.
sp.p: We'll beat 'un like a sheaf o' corn.

sp.speaker: Stock.
sp.p: As I'm a man of truth, he whom you seek is not here.

sp.speaker: All.
sp.p: Where be sister ? where be sister, then !

sp.speaker: Haynes.
sp.p: (to Nell and Moll.) You bean't she—nor—why, no ! (seizing Crowsfoot.) Yes, spite of all her Lunnun rags, I do know her! here she be! here, brothers, be poor lost Susan!

sp.speaker: Crows.
sp.p: (aside.) Susan!

sp.speaker: Haynes, &C.
sp.p: (shaking cudgels at Crowsfoot.) Oh, Susey, Susey!

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: My good people, this lady is a friend of mine—she's not your sister.

sp.speaker: Moll.
sp.p: My own cousin!

sp.speaker: Haynes.
sp.p: (to Crowsfoot.) Deny thine own flesh, and blood ! (they all seize Crowsfoot.)

sp.speaker: Crows.
sp.p: Murder !

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: Mercy ! you'll kill the young woman.

div.stage: Enter Charles and Berkley, R. 1 E.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Shame, friends! What cudgel a woman? (Haynes takes off Crowsfoot's head-dress.) The counsellor! let me congratulate you, learned .sir.

sp.speaker: Crows.
sp.p: Congratulate?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: On your new silk gown. Never did promotion sit so gracefully.

sp.speaker: Crows.
sp.p: (tearing off gown) To bo gulled—robbed! To pay for suppers—

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Four pounds and—Gregory, return the amount. (Berkeley puts purse into Crowsfoot's hand.)

sp.speaker: Crows.
sp.p: lf I could persuade her to—Nelly —Nelly. (Nell turns away.) Tricked—exposed! (all laugh) I'll wage war war with all womankind —I'll confine my practice to suits against 'em, and spend the rest of my days in persecuting the frailties of the whole sex. Oh, woman! woman!

div.stage: Runs off, R. 1 E.—the Party retire up —Moll sits at table, drinking.

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: Ha, ha! Poor counsellor. Now to make my escape.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: What, Nelly, art running after the lawyer?

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: Indeed, you must not detain me.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Why, then, I see it; thou'rt an antiquarian in love, and fairly taken with the last century. In truth, now, where would'st go ?

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: In truth, to the theatre. You'll never guess for what ? I am to speak the prologue. Let me go, I pray !

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: You shall go, and I will be at the theatre too.

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: (R.) Yet I'm so frightened !

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Never fear; you may see a friend there, be certain, you may ; and, with such assurance, kind-hearted, good- natured, sprightly Nelly, fare ye well. Fortune plays a blind game, or she had taken better care of you. But, courage! I tell you I and some friends will be at the house.

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: Why, is't a holiday with the Mercer's company ?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Nelly, if thou should'st see me, yet seeing, miss the mercer, then—

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: Must I die for the loss? What then?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p:
Then, own with mighty John, that
" Princes may retire whene'er they please,
" And breathe free air from out their palaces,
" They go sometimes unknown to shun their state,
" And then 'tis manners not to know or wait. "

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: What is all this?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Four lines from the new play to night; mark; them, and learn the wisdom they advise. And so, again, courage, Newlly, courage and success!

div.stage: Exeunt Charles and Berkeley, R.1 E.

sp.speaker: Haynes.
sp.p: And now to return our dresses here to the wardrobe, for again I am one of the theatre.

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: What! you?

sp.speaker: Haynes.
sp.p: By royal mandate from the king. Let's haste; for to day their majesties in full state do honour to Mr Dryden's new play.

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: Lud a mercy! "The Conquest of Grenada? "

sp.speaker: Haynes.
sp.p: The same.

sp.speaker: Moll.
sp.p: (staggering forward, L.) Why, bless me, child! Thou'rt white as chalk!

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: Well I may be—I have to speak the prologue ; and before the King and Queen! My gracious!

sp.speaker: Haynes.
sp.p: (C.) What are you the new comer the managers are mad about ? You the heroine of the great hat ?

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: I! Manager Hart would make me promise. But I have been so teazed! I have hardly looked at the words. What shall I do?

sp.speaker: Haynes.
sp.p: Hope, and all will be well.

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.p: Say you so? Why, then, good friends come to the theatre, and hear me, if there yet be time, rehearse the prologue.

div.stage: Exit R. 1 E.

sp.speaker: Moll.
sp.p: Joseph—Mr. Haynes—you brought me here—pray take me back again.

div.stage: (Haynes leads Moll off—all Exeunt, R. 1 E.

div.head: SCENE 2.—A lobby of the King's Theatre, (1st grooves.)
div.stage: Enter Hart in full dress, L.

sp.speaker: Hart.
sp.p: (looking at watch.) If the girl, this Nell Gwynne should disappoint us after all!

div.stage: Enter Major Mohun, L.

sp.speaker: Mohun.
sp.p: My dear Hart, the girl is come! Though, indeed, half dead with fear for the event.

sp.speaker: Hart.
sp.p: We'll have her run through the verse at once. Where's Dryden ?

sp.speaker: Mohun.
sp.p: He's behind, admiring the big hat; and, with the prophetic fury of a poet, vows it will extinguish Nokes for ever. In sober truth, 'tis a beaver for Atlas.

div.stage: Enter Joe Haynes, L.

sp.speaker: Hart.
sp.p: Mr. Haynes, you are welcome once again ; yet mind, no more bells, Joe. But time hastens ; let us go and encourage the new comer.

div.stage: (flourish of trumpets without.

sp.speaker: Mohun.
sp.p: Their majesties!

sp.speaker: Hart.
sp.p:
Nay, then, we must even trust to fortune; for there
is no-time for rehearsal. Away, gentlemen; away, all to your places! Come, Mohun to light the king to his box.

div.stage: (Flourish of trumpets. Exeunt Mohun with Hart - and Haynes, L. Trumpets,and music continued, when)—

Re-enter Hart and Mohun lighting Charles and the Queen, with Guards, Lords, and Ladies in waiting, Attendants, &c. &c.; they cross the stage, L. Procession—Four Yeomen of Guard with halberds. Hart and Mohun with lighted candles bowing on King Charles, leading the Queen. —Berkeley—Four Ladies—Four Lords—Four Yeomen of Guards two and two—they go off, R.

div.head: SCENE 3.—Interior of King's Theatre.
div.stage: The royal box, in which are their Majesties, &c. Music, "Britons Strike Home."
The bell is rung, the curtains open in the middle, and enter Nell "in a broad-brimmed hat and waist belt."

sp.speaker: Nell.
sp.l: " This jest was first of the other house's making;
" And, five times tried, has never failed of taking:
" For, 'twere a shame a poet should be killed
" Under the shelter of so broad a shield—
" This is that hat whose very sight did win ye
" To laugh and clap as though the devil were in ye ;
" As then for Nokes, so now I hope you'll be
" So dull to laugh once more for love of me.
"I'll write— (recognizing Charles.) What! he—the King! the words are flown.
For Dryden's syllables, pray take my own. (let's hat fall.)
First let me ask that niceness may not halt
With eager eyes, to scan out every fault;
And miss, with venal look, those streaks of light,
Which fortune only would not have more bright.
Of good and ill all character is made ;
The good accept—the rest cast into shade.
Of some we'd shew (if so our hopes might draw, )
The moral amber, with nor grub nor straw ;
Would take away th' unseemly gnats and flies,
And keep the prettiness that glads all eyes :
This our design ; if granted, may I ask
Your hands and wishes for th' attempted task ?

div.stage: Band plays " Britons Strike Home,"
Curtain
